title: բարձ
etymology: = Iranian loanword. cognate with sans. barhis "a cushion for sitting on during sacrifices for the gods and sacrificers", eng. barəziš "pillow, cover", oss. baz "pillow". European cognates of these words are: hrus. bal-sinis "pillow", pobalso "head pillow, snare", gal. bulga "purse", serb. blazina "bedspread", blazinja "pillow", hbg. bolstar, eng. bolster, hhyus. bolstr, ger. polster "pillow" etc. (see Hick l2, p. 492 Kluge 374), whose proto-form is hkh. bhelg'h. Arm. bardz cannot be native, because hkh. Against the form bhelg'h we would have *reghdz, or if we assume that Armenian originated from the lower consonant of the hnkh. bhjg'h, then we would have *bağdz. at least always with gh and not with eh. The latter is evidence of Iranian borrowing. In Persian itself there is no form barz, but we have only prs. [arabic word] bāliš or [arabic word] bālīn, pl. bāusn kurd. bālišt, bālišne, bā̄līv, bālge, vākh baleš, sar. balax (see Horn, Grdr. § 172). However, the form *barz must have existed in antiquity, from which the word bardz is derived (cf. Hübsh. Pers. Stud. 221-3). According to Lidén, Arm. Stud. 44, the same word is also known as Persian bai "wing, animal's tail" <*bard < *barz, by which the other meanings of the Armenian word (head pillow and body pillow) are found in Persian. (See Pokorny 2, 182). Hubsh. I Anz. 10, 45. / [Non-English or unclear etymology removed.].

title: գիշեր
etymology: = Native Armenian word, whose cognates are Greek: ἔσπερος “evening”, τα ἔοπερα “evening time”, later ήεσ-πέρα “evening”, Lat. vesper “evening”, ves-bera “evening time”, French: vèpres “evening prayer”, Hirsh: fescor, Cymraeg: ucher, Lithuanian: vākaras “evening”, yakaraī “west”, Lat. vakars “evening”, Hsl: večerù, Russian: вeчepь “evening”, вeqepя “dinner”, German: Vesperbrot “evening bread, dinner”, borrowed from Hungarian: vacsora “dinner” (Walde, 827, Boisacq, 288, Trautmann, 348). These words are prefixed by the Greek: ἀνθρωπος vespero, veqero, but the phonetic differences are not fully explained. Armenian, which comes from the ancient *geşer form, has the proto-form veqero in Armenian. cf. dog, esh. -Toxer has the form wse, which is the same as kuch. yasl "night" (Pokorny, 1, 15 and 311, Kluge, 510, Ernout-Meillet, 1054). -Hyupsh. 435. / [Non-English or unclear etymology removed.].

title: գիրք
etymology: From Old Armenian book (girkʻ), plural of gir (gir, 'writing'). From Proto-Indo-European *gʷriH- ('to praise, to sing'). Cognates include Sanskrit गिर् (gir, 'song, praise'), Avestan 𐬔𐬀𐬌𐬭𐬌- (gairi-, 'song').

title: երգ
etymology: + Native Armenian word. cf. sans. [other alphabet] arká “song, music”, (k (gen. rčás) “foot-worn, striped”, [other alphabet] arč, archati “sings praise, greets, honors”. This root is not preserved in other languages, therefore it is unrelated to lt. loquor “to speak”, gn. λαϰ︎ ἐλαϰαν, λέληϰα “to speak, shout” and so on (see Walde, 440). Pokorny, 1, 147 puts the proto-form in the Hn. erk- “to radiate, to sound like a tree”, while the first meanings of the words sans. arká, r'k “song, music” are “ray, flash of lightning, sun, fire”. -Hyubsh. 443. / [Non-English or unclear etymology removed.].

title: ծղնի
etymology: A [[ghost word]] found in Norayr's French–Armenian dictionary<ref></ref> and from there copied into later dictionaries.<ref></ref><ref></ref> In his unfinished Middle Armenian dictionary, Norayr quotes the term in an unpublished passage by a certain Tēr Komitas who probably should be identified with [[w:hy:Komitas Kyomurchyan|]] (17th c.) of Constantinople, the brother of Likely borrowed from or a related word, because of the legend that the halcyons calm the waters, but the details are unclear.

title: կարմիր
etymology: -Phl. *karmir from a synonym that was not preserved in Iranian literature and only appeared in the upper part (see Gauthiot, Gram, sogd. 143 and MSL 17, 247) in the form of Sogdian krm'yr (read karmir) "red". (But according to Marquardt (personal) there is a Phl. [Syriac word] ︎ kalmīr "red"). Also borrowed from Noon is the Hebrew word karmil "the red color of the worm and the thing dyed with it". cf. also Sans. kirmira. The root of all is the Persian [Arabic word] kirm, Znd. kərəma (?), Tans. krmi "worm", whose cognates are Albanian. krum, krimp, Irish. cruim, Lithuanian. kirmis, Hebrew. girmis "worm", the common proto-form is Hn. q'rmi-: The reason for the development of the meaning "worm> red" is that in ancient times the red dye of apricot was prepared from a type of worm (coccus ilicis or coccus polo-hicus), which was a fungus growing on oak trees. Cf. from other languages with a similar development of meaning: Greek ϰὄϰϰος "worm" and ϰὸϰϰινος "red", Greek čirmi "worm" and čruminu "red", Latin ver-mis "worm" and vermiculus "worm">Italian vermiglio, French vermeil "red", Greek *v'rmi "worm" and Ukrainian vermjanyj "red", Hebrew wormyan "red". Then the Iranian word spread to the East and West (it entered Europe through the Arabs) Greek Türk. [arabic word] qərməz "son", qər-məzə "red", Kurdish. kərməz, Arabic. kir-miz, kirmizi, Spanish. alquermes, French. μermè᾽ etav. carmesino. German. Karmesin, Polish. kiermes, alkiermes, Bulgarian. hrimiz, Serbian. grimis, Russian. кармазинъ, Middle Armenian. хүрмзи, Һүрмзи "apricot", used later in the Ramic language, as. Kost. erzn. pp. 114, 120 ff. (Boisacq 245, 844, Berne-ker 169, 490).-Hyubsh. 167: / [Non-English or unclear etymology removed.].

title: վաշտ
etymology: = Must be borrowed from a word meaning "army" in Phl. *vašk. Such a word is not preserved in Iranian literature, but there is Phl. gast (old vašt) "became", vaštan "to become", which on the basis of the Iranian rt>št sound change gives rise to vartītan, gartītan "to become", vartisn, gartišn "twist, circle", Sans. vart, vartate "to become", Prs. [arabic word] gar-dīdan "to turn, to turn, to turn", [arabic word] gāštan "to turn", [arabic word] gardana> from the word гртнак (=lt. verto "to turn", Old High German werdan, German werden "to become, to be", R. вepηetь "to turn, to turn, to turn, to pierce", вepηeл "skewer", Lit. verčiu, versti "to turn", etc.). The root of all is Hn. vert- "to turn, to make" (Pokorny 1, 274, Horn § 886). The words denoting "group" in general are related to the idea of "all". qu. regiment, bar, battalion, camp ("maneak. 2. army" <pl. čambar "circle"), Tchk top, lt. circulus, fr. cercle, rus. круръ, коужокъ, following which also the working circle "клорак, тэмек. 2. group of people".-Ач. / [Non-English or unclear etymology removed.].

title: ցախ
etymology: =A native Armenian word, the antecedent of which (at least in terms of the initial sound) is imprecise. The cognates are stns. çákha-, prs. [arabic word] ︎ sax lit. šaká, hsl. sakù, kymr cainc, mirl. gēc, nirl. gēag "branch, twig", lit., sakēsis, hprus. sagnis, lett. sakne "root", goth. hōha. "stem" and perhaps hsl. soxa, bulg. sochá, rus. coxá "rod, stick, stem" (Pokorny 1, 335, Trautmann 297). The antecedent of these is k'ak, which also has its own rang-gai form k'ank. For Armenian, Bar-tbolomae Stud. 2, 41 assumes hnkh. k'hakhā, Meillet MSL 8, 294 i 9. 323 The word assumes two forms, one of which is represented by Armenian and Persian, and the other by Sanskrit. Finally Petersson, Ar. u. Arm Stud. 98 places the Armenian proto-form as хнх. sk'ə-kho.-Хүбш. 499. / [Non-English or unclear etymology removed.].

title: լաթ
etymology: = Pos. [arabic word] lat "part, piece. 2. canvas, cloth", with which it is the same as Sans. la-ta "cloth, garment", borrowed from Iranian by the Sun. Turk. [arabic word] latta "piece of cloth, a kind of rag", chaghat. latta "rug, old and worn-out cloth", ut. lat same note. From these, in my opinion, were also borrowed Russian. lata "piece of cloth", латать "outdated, worn-out cloth, rag", ла́тать "to patch", Ukrainian. latka "rug", Czech. látati "to patch", látka "pattern, cloth", Polish. laty "tsyn-Radim dictionary-17 tsot". (Berneker 693 mistakenly considers these words to be native Slavic, and this is why he does not find cognate forms in other Indo-European languages). - Ach. / [Non-English or unclear etymology removed.].

title: եղունգն
etymology: -A native Armenian word, whose other Indo-European counterparts are: sans. [other alphabet] nakhá-, phl. prs. [arabic word] naxun, kurd. neinuk. afghan. nuk, beluchi. nākun, nāhun, hindu. naxun, gypsy. nati, nai, yun. ὄνυς, ὄντχος, lt. un-guis, ungula, hbg. nagal, ger. nagel, eng. noezel, hhyus. nagl, eng. nail, hprus. nagutis, lit. nagutis, nāgas, na-gá, lett. nags, hsl. nogūti, rus. ноготъ, hirl. ingen, mirl. inga, kymr. eguin, corn. ewin, brt. ivin, all "nail". From the Hawaiian form are hprus. nage, hsl. noga, Czech noha, Russian нora, which mean "foot" (Walde, 851, Boisacq, 7C5, Trautmann, 192, Horn § 1016). It is very difficult to connect these apparently cognate words from a phonetic point of view and to reveal the Indo-European proto-form. The Baltic-Slavic forms give naga and nagut, the Germanic languages give nogh-lo-or nokhlo-(Kluge, 344), Boisacq gives the common [Hebrew word] l, nagh, */, nogh forms - the latter especially for the Aryan languages. It is more difficult to translate Armenian, the closest forms of which are the Latin and Germanic forms. the first with its u vowel, the others with their corresponding consonants (n, g, 1). In Armenian, of course, the initial e is added because of gh, and the final is partial. Thus the radical remains *ghung <*lung. This is yours in ancient Armenian (Pokorny, 1, 180-2, Ernout-Meillet, 1083). / [Non-English or unclear etymology removed.].

title: ԱՐՈՅՐ
etymology: = From the Old Phl. *rōδ word (compare with the word boyr< nd. baoiδi-), as Hprs. *rauδa, against which the Iranian forms Phl. rōd, rōi, Prs. [arabic word] roy "tin, copper", Reluj. rōd and Sozd. rōή "copper" are alive. Cognate with these are Sans. lōha- "red metal, copper, iron", Hsl. ruda "metal", Lat. raudus, rodus, rudus "copper piece", Hbg. aruzzi, erizzi, Germ. Erz "tin copper", Engl. lëad, Dutch. lood "lead" etc., these are derived from the Hn. reud, reudh or leud forms (Walde, p. 643, Horn § 635, but Kluge 123). If this word were native Armenian and not borrowed, we would have the form *aroyt, *aroyd, *eroyt, *eroyd or *loyt. -Sumerian also has similarities with the Indo-European word. urud asar-urudū “copper”. -Hyubsh. 111.

title: ԲԱՐՁ
etymology: = Iranian loanword. cognate with sans. barhis "a cushion for sitting on during sacrifices for the gods and sacrificers", eng. barəziš "pillow, cover", oss. baz "pillow". European cognates of these words are: hrus. bal-sinis "pillow", pobalso "head pillow, snare", gal. bulga "purse", serb. blazina "bedspread", blazinja "pillow", hbg. bolstar, eng. bolster, hhyus. bolstr, ger. polster "pillow" etc. (see Hick l2, p. 492 Kluge 374), whose proto-form is hkh. bhelg'h. Arm. bardz cannot be native, because hkh. Against the form bhelg'h we would have *reghdz, or if we assume that Armenian originated from the lower consonant of the hnkh. bhjg'h, then we would have *bağdz. at least always with gh and not with eh. The latter is evidence of Iranian borrowing. In Persian itself there is no form barz, but we have only prs. [arabic word] bāliš or [arabic word] bālīn, pl. bāusn kurd. bālišt, bālišne, bā̄līv, bālge, vākh baleš, sar. balax (see Horn, Grdr. § 172). However, the form *barz must have existed in antiquity, from which the word bardz is derived (cf. Hübsh. Pers. Stud. 221-3). According to Lidén, Arm. Stud. 44, the same word is also known as Persian bai "wing, animal's tail" <*bard < *barz, by which the Armenian word's other meanings (head pillow and body pillow) are derived in Persian. (See Pokorny 2, 182). Hubsh. I Anz. 10, 45.

title: ԲՈՅԺ
etymology: -Ph,. bož-form. hmmt. znd. buǰ, ba-og-«to dissolve (the body), to cleanse, to free, to save», baoxtar «liberator, savior». buxti «liberation, salvation», pazend. boxtan «to free, to save, to free», božisn «liberation, salvation», buxt-ruānī «salvation of the soul», phl. bōxtan «liberate, save», bōxtār «savior», Baluch. bó-jag, bōžaγ «to dissolve, to open, to break» (Bar-tholomae 916-17, Pokorny 2, 145). The proto-form of all is hnkh. bheug(h)-«to remove, to escape, to be freed». Armenian is borrowed from Iranian, as the j sound shows and has no connection with the word doctor. The cognates of the Iranian words are according to Walde 326: Lt. fungor “to perform, to finish” (as if by performing the work to free. cf. Rmk. khalysil “to finish” <Arab. xalas “freedom, salvation”), Goth. us-bau-gjan “to sweep, to wipe”, Pali pari-bhuñǰati “to clean, to wipe”, vi-nib-bhuǰati “to divide”. -Hyupsh. 122.

title: ԲՈՒԹ
etymology: = Native Armenian word from the Hn. bhut-form. The same meaning as Armenian is Dutch bot, Danish but, North German butt “but (literally and or figuratively, that is, “big-headed, stupid”) short and stout”, German verbutet “physically or mentally undeveloped” Greek buttet “short and stout”, Butz “short and stout man, animal or beast”, loanwords: hung, buta “butt, idiot”, Spanish port. boto, botoso “butt”, French (pied) bot “crouched (foot)”, Walloon bott, Italian boto, Romanian but. butacīū “butt”, Russian botètь, butetь “to grow fat”, Slovak buta “big-headed man, stupid man”. According to Berneker 77, the Slavic group of these words is borrowed from German, the origin of the others is not clear. According to Pokorny 2, 127, the Germanic group also comes from the root bhaud-, bhūd- "to push, to push", the cognate of which is bhaut-, bhūt- "to push, to push" (Po-korny 2, 125). In the form of the latter, and in meaning to the former, it becomes the middle form of the two, let us add especially the Greek btel, bo-tel "to push, to push", which also retains the verb meaning of the same roots. -Ach.

title: ԳԱՅԼ
etymology: = Native Armenian word, whose cognates are: Sans. vrka-, Znd. vəhrka-, Ps. ❇ gurg, Goth. vulfs, Germ. Wolf, Hsl. vluku, Rus.-волкъ, Lit. vitkas, Hrus. vilkis, Lat. wIlks, Irl. fael, Hisl. ylgr. Albanian. ul'k, Jn. λόϰος, Lat. lupus (Fr. loup, It. lupa, lupo, ulivella, Rom. lup, Esp. lo-bo), borrowed from Slavic and even Mordvin. virgas, all with the meaning of "wolf" (Walde, 447, Boisacq, 5аn Trautmann. 359, Horn, § 910, Kluge, 535). The Armenian proto-form has been suggested in various ways. Hubschmann puts the Hn. vlyo-, Bartholomae, Stud. II. 13 khkh. vlk2y-, Fick, II. 259 khkh. vailo. It is certain, however, that this word, having been subjected to ta-bou, like the word arj, has become the subject of voluntary transformation. The Indo-Europeanists, analyzing the proto-form of the word "wolf" (ul-qu) and connecting it with the pre-form of the word "fox" (allegedly ul-p-), believe that the simple root of both is uel "to prey" (cf. Lt. vello "to snatch", voltur "to snatch a bird", lacer "to tear, to tear), by which "gall" means "predator" (see Pokorny, 1, 213 and 316, Ernout-Meillet, 539). Hubsh. 431.

title: ԳԱՐՈՒՆ
etymology: = Native Indo-European Armenian word, whose cognates are sans. [other alphabet] vasantā, znd. vanhar-, vañhri, hprs. vahara, phl. vahār, prs. [arabic word] bahār, oss. valag, gypsy. varno, yn. ἔαρ, ἠρ, homer. fέαρ, hsl. vesna, rus. вecнa "spring", lit. vasará "part". lt. vēr "spring", rum. vará "summer", hhys. vár, hisl. vār, old cymru. guiannuin, new cymru. gwanwуn, corn. guaintoin "year". The first Indo-European proto-form was uèsr, with which there was also uēsr and the shortened vēr from this. The root of the word was rln, that is, ugh. ueser, gen. uesnes. The final r of the word was replaced by n in some, and in this way many of the above forms arose (see Walde, 819, Boisacq, 210, Horn, § 243, Trautmann, 356, Pokorny, 1, 310, Ernout-Meillet, 1045). - For the explanation of the Armenian word, it is first necessary to determine the root form. - The names of the seasons have influenced each other. Thus, tmarn-amtotn, zinnarn-zinmeran have the same form and their form of conjugation has influenced the irregular form of gar-nan-ashnan. The orthographic forms are also the same: garun and ashun. So, which of these has influenced which, in other words, in the word zarun -un belongs to the root. It is likely that the first, with which the root would become *gar. and this form could be connected with the ancient form vesr, passing through the forms vesar>*gehar >*za-har >*gar. The first of these is already found in Greek (fέαρ< vesar), the second in Lithuanian, the third is similar to the Hebrew and Phl. forms, and the fourth (*gar) has reached the Hist. vār form. -Hyupsh. 432.

title: ԳԵՐԱՆ
etymology: =-A native Armenian word, from the Old Norse vernnā-form, whose cognates are found only in Celtic languages and Albanian. Thus: Brit. gwern and M. Brit. guernenn "mast. 2. alder. 3. alder forest", Corn. guern "mast", gwern-en "alder", Cym. cмвrn "alder forest, marshy place", gwern-en "ship mast, alder" Myrl. fern, fernog "alder", New Irish. fearn "mast, alder" (from this are Piedmontese verna, Provençal verna, verno, French verne, vergne "alder"), Albanian verε "white alder, populus Alba". The proto-form of Celtic words is *yer-nā, which also gives Albanian. For the use of the name of the tree as a general meaning of "log", cf. Tch. perdu and Gb. pardu "log", which come from the Greek word barti (see also), Pokorny, 1, 292.

title: ԳԻՆԻ
etymology: = Native Armenian word. Derived from the original form ge-ni, the mother of which is the ancient voinio-compare with it. ln. οϊνος "wine" (<foῖνος, Hessian γοϊνος, Thracian γάνος), οἰνη (Dor. ανα) "vine", οἰνας, "vine, wine", Hat. gens (š is a participle of the direct object) "wine", Albanian vεnε, Tosk. vere "wine" (<*voinā), Lat. vīnum (<*voino-) "wine", from which Rom. vinū, Ital. Spanish vino, French vin. Borrowed from Latin are Goth. wein, Hungarian win, German Wein, English vine, Irish fin, Welsh gwin, -borrowed from German are Hsl. vino, Russian вино, from which are Lit. vynas "wine" and Mordvin. (non-Indo-European) vīnā "wine, vodka". The same word is also found in Semitic languages. e.g. Hebrew word yain (< wain), Arabic word wain, Ethiopian. ⦿P︎ wein, Assyrian inu, in the Semitic proto-language wainu "wine" (Walde, 839, Boisacq, 691, Kluge, 523). -The origin of the word wine and with it the original homeland of the grape, as well as the origin, development and spread of the craft of winemaking, has been much debated. Long studies have been carried out by Schröder, Hehn, Hommel, Kremer, Jensen, etc. Some of them believe that wine was originally Indo-European and that the Semites learned winemaking from them, and therefore the Roman forms are a loan from the Indo-Europeans. According to Lag. Arm. Stud. p. 35 note. From Cyprus it passed to the Jews and from them to the Arabs. Others, on the contrary, argue that wine was originally Semitic, from them it passed to the Indo-Europeans by loan. The most accurate is (as Meil. let, MSL, 15, 163 and others accept) that wine is the invention and cultivation of the ancient urban-dwelling people of the Mediterranean, which later, on the one hand, passed by borrowing to the Semites and on the other hand, to the neighboring Indo-Europeans (the same, Armenian. Albanian) and through them gradually spread further afield (Latin, German, Celtic, Slavonic). It entered Germany through the Latins. From there it later passed to the Slavs. It spread through the Armenians to the Caucasus.-(Pokornv, 1 226, Ernout-Meillet, 1068).-Hibsh. 434.

title: ԳԻՇԵՐ
etymology: = Native Armenian word, whose cognates are Greek: ἔσπερος “evening”, τα ἔοπερα “evening time”, later ήεσ-πέρα “evening”, Lat. vesper “evening”, ves-bera “evening time”, French: vèpres “evening prayer”, Hirsh: fescor, Cymraeg: ucher, Lithuanian: vākaras “evening”, yakaraī “west”, Lat. vakars “evening”, Hsl: večerù, Russian: вeчepь “evening”, вeqepя “dinner”, German: Vesperbrot “evening bread, dinner”, borrowed from Hungarian: vacsora “dinner” (Walde, 827, Boisacq, 288, Trautmann, 348). These words are prefixed by the Greek: ἀνθρωπος vespero, veqero, but the phonetic differences are not fully clarified. Armenian, which comes from the ancient *geshesher form, has the proto-form veqero in Armenian. cf. dog, esh. -Tokharenian has the form wse, with which it is the same as kuch. yasl "night" (Pokorny, 1, 15 and 311, Kluge, 510, Ernout-Meillet, 1054). -Hyubsh. 435.

title: ԴՄԱԿ
etymology: = Phl. from the synonym *dumak. This form is not preserved in Iranian literature, but there are many of its cognates, as. znd. duma-, Phl. dum, dumb, dumbak, dunbak, dunb, prs. ❇ dum, dumm, [arabic word] dunb, [arabic word] dunba, [arabic word] dunbāl., Baluch. dunbag, Kurdish. dunk, duw. Afghan. lam, Minj. ləm, Oss. dumág, də-mag “dumbak” (Horn, § 573). The proto-form of Iranian words is according to Bartholomae, Stud. II. 101 *dhumbhma-. Also borrowed from Iranian are Assyrian. [syriac word] ︎ dunbā “tail”, Georgian. დუპა დუპა “dumbak oil”, დუპი dumi “dumbak sheep”. Pokorny, 1, 816, from the Iranian forms kzum hbg. zumpfo, mbg. zumpi "male". The preposition is put in hkh. dumb-(or dumbh-?) "male, tail" and perhaps originally "rod, scepter". -Hyubsh. 144.

title: ԴՈՆ
etymology: = Native Armenian word, from the Hm. dhon-form, its closest descendants are Lit. duona "bread" and Lat. duōna "piece of bread", the proto-form of which Trautmann, 58 places döná-"bread". On the second line, parallel to this are Sans. dhānás "grain, wheat", dhānyá-"grain", Phl. dān, Prs. [arabic word] dān or [arabic word] dāna "grain, grain, grain" (from which are borrowed Hm. Gur. dan "grain of wheat, grain", Georgian. dana dana "grain, grain", Arab. [arabic word] danaǰ, Kurdish. dané, Turkish. dane, tane), Baluch. dān, dānag, Afghan, dane "ha-tikō (Horn, § 535). In Zandkern, where the word would have had the form *dānā-, it is preserved only in danō-karš-bard, which means "a kind of ant that pulls grains" (Bartholomae, 734). On the third line, they are probably compared with Lat. fēlix "fertile, fruitful. 2. rich. 3. happy", fēnus "harvest from the soul", fēcundus "fertile, fertile", fētus "fertilized, pregnant", Myrl. indile "growth, crop", Hbrēt-endlim "harvest", Cym. ynnnil, ynnil "profit, gain, benefit", the root of which is Hn. dhē- "land, the product of the soil, gain" (see Walde, 280). Pokorny, 1, 831 lit. duona "bread" is separated from this group and placed in duoti from the verb "to eat". However, Trautmann, 58, does not do so. -Ach.

title: ԵՂՈՒՆԳՆ
etymology: -A native Armenian word, whose other Indo-European counterparts are: sans. [other alphabet] nakhá-, phl. prs. [arabic word] naxun, kurd. neinuk. afghan. nuk, beluchi. nākun, nāhun, hindu. naxun, gypsy. nati, nai, yun. ὄνυς, ὄντχος, lt. un-guis, ungula, hbg. nagal, ger. nagel, eng. noezel, hhyus. nagl, eng. nail, hprus. nagutis, lit. nagutis, nāgas, na-gá, lett. nags, hsl. nogūti, rus. ноготъ, hirl. ingen, mirl. inga, kymr. eguin, corn. ewin, brt. ivin, all "nail". From the Hawaiian form are hprus. nage, hsl. noga, Czech noha, Russian нora, which mean "foot" (Walde, 851, Boisacq, 7C5, Trautmann, 192, Horn § 1016). It is very difficult to connect these apparently cognate words from a phonetic point of view and to reveal the Indo-European proto-form. The Baltic-Slavic forms give naga and nagut, the Germanic languages give nogh-lo-or nokhlo-(Kluge, 344), Boisacq gives the common [Hebrew word] l, nagh, */, nogh forms - the latter especially for the Aryan languages. It is more difficult to translate Armenian, the closest forms of which are the Latin and Germanic forms. the first with its u vowel, the others with their corresponding consonants (n, g, 1). In Armenian, of course, the initial e is added, due to gh, and the final vowel is partial. Thus, the radical part remains *ghung <*lung. This is what you have in ancient Armenian (Pokorny, 1, 180-2, Ernout-Meillet, 1083).

title: ԵՐԳ
etymology: + Native Armenian word. cf. sans. [other alphabet] arká “song, music”, (k (gen. rčás) “foot-worn, striped”, [other alphabet] arč, arcati “sings praise, greets, honors”. This root is not preserved in other languages, therefore it is unrelated to lt. loquor “to speak”, yn. λαϰ︎ ἐλαϰαν, λέληϰα “to speak, shout” and so on (see Walde, 440). Pokorny, 1, 147 puts the proto-form in hn. erk- “to radiate, to sound like a tree”, while sans. arká, r'k “song, music” have the first meanings of “ray, flash of lightning, sun, fire”. -Hyubsh. 443.

title: ԻՇԽԵԼ
etymology: = Iranian. xši-"to rule" borrowed from the form. cf. znd. xši-"to rule, to dominate, to be powerful, to be able", xšaya-"mighty, able, prince, princedom", Hebrew. xšāyaϑiya-"king", xšaϑra-"state", Sogdian. çšεyan "king" (Gauthiot, Gr. sogd. 97), *xšyay "to command, to dominate", Man. xšāwn, xšiwn "prince, king" (ИАН 1907, 542-3), Phl. prs. šah "king". with which are the same Sans. kši, kšá yati "to rule, to have, to be lord", kšatrá-"princedom", Yng. ϰτάομαι “to acquire, to receive”, ϰτήμα “something, wealth”, ϰτήνεα “something, wealth, herd, cattle or sheep”, ph. qthē (Boisacq 523-4, Walde 680, Pokorny 1, 504). With the same Iranian words one can now compare Hat. išha-“lord” (Hrozny, Die Spra-che der Hethiter, p. 226). In Armenian, the Iranian [arabic word] has been transformed according to the law into shkh (cf. vashkh <phl. vaxš, boshkhnel -baoxšna-, e-rashkh<raxš, разбайнгел<baxš etc.). but since there is no word in Armenian that begins with shkh-vowel, the e-na-kha vowel has been added. (cf. ashkhar <xšaϑra-, ash-khet <*xšaita-, in which the addition is a. but here it is e, which is the equivalent of a (cf. arag-erag) and because of the following j it became e. as we have esh <*egh<*ek'vo), thus the proto-form of the Armenian word is *eshkhel, which later, according to the laws of Armenian phonetics, became iskhel.

title: ԼԱԹ
etymology: = Pos. [arabic word] lat "part, piece. 2. canvas, cloth", with which it is the same as Sans. la-ta "cloth, garment", borrowed from Iranian by the Sun. Turk. [arabic word] latta "piece of cloth, a kind of rag", chaghat. latta "rug, old and worn-out cloth", ut. lat same note. From these, in my opinion, were also borrowed Russian. lata "piece of cloth", латать "outdated, worn-out cloth, rag", ла́тать "to patch", Ukrainian. latka "rug", Czech. látati "to patch", látka "pattern, cloth", Polish. laty "tsyn-Radim dictionary-17 tsot". (Berneker 693 mistakenly considers these words to be native Slavic, and this is why he does not find cognate forms in other Indo-European languages.) - Ach.

title: ԿԱՆԵՓ
etymology: = A word common in Indo-European and non-Indo-European languages. From the a-rajins we have Persian [arabic word] kanab [arabic word] kanav, Greek ϰάνναβις, ϰάνναβος (used in Herodotus, D. 74), Latvian cannabis (>Italian canapa, canape, Romanian kanabiu, konabiu, French chanvre), English haenep, Hebrew hampr, English hemp, Hungarian hanai, German Hanf, Dutch hennep, Hebrew knapios, Lithuanian kanāpés, Hebrew konop-lja, Russian конопля, кононо, Polish konop, Albanian kanəp, kərp, etc. From the non-Indo-European kans we have Mordovian kane'f, kanjtf, Turkish kenevir, Hungarian kanaf, Assyrian. [syriac word] qənapā, arab. [arabic word] qinnab. a-sur. qanapu, qanabu, qunupu and others. These words are not derived from a tribal, but from an oriental language. Schrader, Reallex. and Hehn, Kulturpfl p. 188 want to place the parent source in the Finnish-Syrian Dictionary-33 *kana-pis complex. cf. cheremis. ken'e, kin'e "cane" and Syrian-Vot-yak piš, puš "cane, cone". The word passed through the Scythians and across southern Russia to the Germans on one side and the Greeks on the other. From the Greeks it passed to the Latins and from the Germans to the Slavs, from whom to the Lithuanians. A kind of alcoholic drink was made from hemp, which was the predecessor of wine. Thus, Sans. çaná-"cane" (which is the same word as hemp, borrowed from India) and Ossetian. san "wine" are related. Perhaps also Sans. bhanga-"cane", Znd. banha-"cane and the intoxicating drink made from it", Russian. peniká, Polish. pienka "cane" originated and inflected from the same word (Walde 123, Boisacq 407, Berneker 559, Kluge 202). As with the above, the original source of the Armenian word is unknown. The closest form is Persian, which does not fully correspond to the Armenian word. The original source of the word Armenian may be Pahlavi, but this form is also unknown.

title: ԿԱՐՄԻՐ
etymology: -Phl. *karmir from a synonym that was not preserved in Iranian literature and only appeared in the upper part (see Gauthiot, Gram, sogd. 143 and MSL 17, 247) in the form of Sogdian krm'yr (read karmir) "red". (But according to Marquardt (personal) there is a Phl. [Syriac word] ︎ kalmīr "red"). Also borrowed from Noon is the Hebrew word karmil "the red color of the worm and the thing dyed with it". cf. also Sans. kirmira. The root of all is the Persian [Arabic word] kirm, Znd. kərəma (?), Tans. krmi "worm", whose cognates are Albanian. krum, krimp, Irish. cruim, Lithuanian. kirmis, Hebrew. girmis "worm", the common proto-form is Hn. q'rmi-: The reason for the development of the meaning "worm> red" is that in ancient times the red dye of apricot was prepared from a type of worm (coccus ilicis or coccus polo-hicus), which was a fungus growing on oak trees. Cf. from other languages with a similar development of meaning: Greek ϰὄϰϰος "worm" and ϰὸϰϰινος "red", Greek čirmi "worm" and čruminu "red", Latin ver-mis "worm" and vermiculus "worm">Italian vermiglio, French vermeil "red", Greek *v'rmi "worm" and Ukrainian vermjanyj "red", Hebrew wormyan "red". Then the Iranian word spread to the East and West (it entered Europe through the Arabs) Greek Türk. [arabic word] qərməz “son”, qər-məzə “red”, Kurdish. kərməz, Arabic. kir-miz, kirmizi, Spanish. alquermes, French. μermè᾽ etav. carmesino. German. Karmesin, Polish. kiermes, alkiermes, Bulgarian. hrimiz, Serbian. grimis, Russian. кармазинъ, Middle Armenian. хүрмзи, Һүрмзи “apricot”, used later in the Ramic language, as. Kost. erzn. pp. 114, 120 ff. (Boisacq 245, 844, Berne-ker 169, 490).-Hyubsh. 167.

title: ԿՈՃ
etymology: POKH.-Georgian. kochi kochi "boys' game stick, ankle, heel, log, horse, a kind of dance holding the knees (присядка)", yokva kochiva "to dance holding the knees", kocha kocha "gingerbread. gingembre". Vviteli kocha gh' vitel'i kocha "gingerbread, ge. yellow ankle", kochaki kutbi "staple, claw", Laz. kochi, konchi "ankle of the foot", tush. kochi "gingerbread", Kurdish. [arabic word] koč “tree stump, root, large piece of wood” (Justi, Dict. Kurde, p. 346. and there p. 80 piš̌koz “button of a dress or flower” is doubtfully compared with the Armenian word button, as Justi does, analyzing piš “front” + Armenian button). Trk. gyr. Evd. ❇, [arabic word] gōjāg “tree button, cocoon” (Yushardzan 330 a and Byur. 1899, 798), gyr. tyrk. and yn. Atn. gōjeg “the wheat taking root under the ground” (Arevelq 1888 Nov. 8-9), ttr. (in the Tobolsk dialect) [arabic word] kučuk "tree buttons" (Будаговъ 2, 146), n. Assyrian. kocá "core (=gyr. cone)", South Russian Gypsy. kučak "button of clothing" (I personally picked this word from the Rostov Gypsies). - I think that also borrowed from Armenian are Türk. [arabic word] qopča "style of clothing", [arabic word] qopčalə "buttoned", [arabic word] qopčalamaq "to fold", which cannot be explained by Turkic roots and for which Будаговъ 2, 68 does not give any Tatar form. (The original Turkish word is [arabic word] duyme "button") Through Turkic the word passed into various languages of Eastern Europe, as. Albanian. kopše, komtše, Romanian copee, Hungarian kapocs, Bulgarian kopca, Serbian kópca, Slovak kgpca same note (Berneker 564). Ut. qoǰ "the sloping beam of a roof".

title: ԿՈՅ
etymology: = Native Armenian word, whose hn. cognates are sans. [other alphabet] gutha-«garbage, excrement». guvati «to take care of natural needs», nd. gū-ϑa-, prs. ❇ gūh, kurd. gū, gau, beluchi giϑ, afghan. γul, hindust. о goh, гн, gus, тохар. kewiye, hsl. govīno, rus. serb. bulgar. говнo, polish. gówno, ukr. b'ivnó, czech. hovno, angsk. cwēad, all «garbage, excrement, dung», with which also lat. bubino «smelly», ukr. hyd «disgust, disgust», czech. o-havny «disgusting», cym. budr "day" (Walde 99, Trautmann 81, Berneker 299, 339, Horn § 947). The Armenian proto-form is not very clear. Bartholomae, Stud. II 28 puts koy <hnkh. govyo-(hmmt. hsl. govino) or gouto-, and ku<hnkh. гүйто-, while the Sans. and Iranian. forms are put hnkh. гүйто-. However, Petersen KZ 39, 383 assumes for the Armenian-Armenian hnkh. gšou-so-. hmmt. goy <hnkh. vose (Pokorny 1, 644-5, rnout-Meillet 115).-Hyubsh. 461.

title: ԿՈՐԿՈՏ
etymology: = Native Armenian word from the root g2rod-, which by repetition gave first *kokrot and by transposition - korkot. as hail<*kakrut<hnkh. gža-g2rodo-. cognate. lit. grudžiū, grudau, grus. ti "to beat barley in a sandpit to make korkot", grudas "grain", lat. grúžu, grudu "to beat in a sandpit", grouds "grain", grūdenes "korkot", graúds "grain", hsl. gruda "hard lump of soil, lump", rus. гpудa "dez", angsk. grut "coarse flour", hhyus. grautr "rough gryus", hbg. gruzzi, mgbg. gruz "grain (of wheat, sand)", hbg. grioz "sand, gravel", hisl. griōt "stones", Eng. grcot "sand", Cym. gro "gravel", Eng. grutze "burr", Hol. qrut, gort "burr", borrowed from German: French gruau "burr", and finally Ger. gross, holl, groot, Eng. great, Eng. great, Hbg. gróz etc., which mean "large, big" (Kluge 191-2, Berneker 357, Trautm. 99). In terms of meaning, these words are completely consistent with Armenian. In terms of form, the Baltic-Slavic group gives grud-, which is also suitable for Armenian. But Walde 661 also assigns to the same group the Latin word grud-. rudus(rodus) "stone-piece", and the root form is *ghreu-, gh-reu-d-, which requires the hy. g. In this case, either the Latin affixation should be deleted (Trautmann does not mention it, and Berneker doubts it) or the change of the degree of the initial vowel should be accepted. Pokorny 1, 648-50 also puts the root form of the affixed words in the anthology of

title: ԿՈՒՄԲ
etymology: = Native Armenian word, from the Old Norse gumb-form, the descendants of which are found only in the Baltic-Slavic family. cognate with Lat. gum-ba “swelling”, Lit. gu bas “a raised swelling or elevation on any manufactured body or also on a stone”, Hsl. ggba Russian. ryóa, Ukrainian. huba, Bulgarian. gъba, Czech. houba, Polish. gabka, Serbian. guba, all of which mean “sponge, mushroom”, the latter also “leprosy, leprosy, scabies”, from which góbav “leper”. The proto-form of all of these is gumba-(Berneker 340, Traut-mann 101) - Pedersen wanted to include in the above family also Greek. σφόγγος, Hy. mushroom, Hebrew. swamb, German. Sehwamm, placing the proto-form of the above sg (h) ombho-. Po-korny 1, 574 places the mentioned words under the root gem- "grasp", but with doubt (as if with a labial suffix). Here one could perhaps also add Norw. kump "ball, lump", dan, kum-pe "ball of flour", Norw. kamp "round stone with tent bumps, mountain peak". Hryv. kgppusteinn "clay stone", Engl. camp "a pile of potatoes, covered with hay and straw, as a winter shelter" and so on. -Ach.

title: ՀՈՒՐ
etymology: = Native Armenian word: from the H. puro-form. Cognates are H. πώρ (gen. πυρός), U. pir, G. Feuer, H. fuir, fur, H. vuur, A. fyr, E. fire, H. furr, fyre, I. ūr, T. por, K. pu. war, K. pahhur, all with the meaning "fire", and C. pyr "hot ashes", G. Russian pyreǰ "depth of the hearth". This word was a form in the Proto-Indo-European language with the exchange of nǰr, from which we also have the same word in the form *hun "fire", from which hunots (see BSL vol. 29, f 87, p. 61). Previously, they thought that the root of the word was pu- "to clean", by which they also attached the Latin purus "pure" to the Old Hungarian form puro. This opinion has now been set aside (Wal-de 626, Boisacq 828, Kluge 139, Traut-mann 232, Pokorny 2, 14). -Hübsch. 469.

title: ՆԱՔԱՐԱԿԻՏ
etymology: = Phl. from an unknown form, with which the Rurrayan Sogdians are identical. nakaratá̄kə (gər- "silver" (see Gauthiot, Gram. sogd. ets 170 and Benyeniste MSL 23, 128). Despite the sequence of the final consonants, the similarity is undeniable. The difference could be explained either by Pah-Lave or by the folk etymology in Armenian (cf. aka-nakit). I do not know whether the above forms are related in any way to the Arabic ︎ nuqra, yg. [arabic word] ︎ nuqār "a piece of molten gold or silver" (Kamus, Turkish transl. B. 131) and [arabic word] naqd "dram", from which rmk. naqd "cash". -Ach.

title: ՈՍԿՐ
etymology: = Native Armenian word, whose cognates are Sans. ásthi Ond. ast, asti-«bone», ascu. «snout, olok», Psr. ast, ustuxvan, Phl. ast Kurd. hesti, hestik, esk, yesk, Oss. stag, štäq (Horn § 81, 85), Eng. ὄστέον «bone». ὄστραϰον «shell», ὄσφός «stiff, stiff», ka-mis haštāi «bone. 2. resistance force» (BSL vol. 38, l 99, p. 139), Lat. os, oss, ossis, Hlt. ossu, ossua, Albanian. ašt, ašte, Cymraeg. ascorn, Brit. askourn (Pokorny 1, 186. Walde 548, Ernout-Meillet 628, Boi-sacq 723, Berneker 583). According to Meillet MSL 23, 259-260, the original form of the word is es, which, due to its extreme shortness, has not been preserved in any language. In Latin, it became oss by the repetition of a consonant. This word, like the words "tongue" and "flower", is one of those compound forms that have undergone very great changes in each language. In many it has received the form ost-, to which new particles have been added. Thus, in Greek. ὄσφός <*ost-bhū, in Cymraeg. ὄσφός, in Corn. ascorn<*ost-cornu. Slavic has added k at the beginning, the origin of which is unclear. Thus, in the case of the word "goat" -koza (Meillet, Rev. des études slaves, 1925, p. 9), which is also found in Greek. καρ. ϰάπρος = lt. aper, gbg. ebur "boar". In Greek, bone implies the proto-form *ostw-er (Meillet, Esq. 14, 28), in which w corresponds to lt. u (ossu, ossua) and is probably also found in the Greek word ὄστέον, where between the sounds ε and o there was s or y or w. The proto-language stw vowel group would be Greek. sk, as also shown by ε<хнх. istwo-form.-Hyupsh. 482.

title: ՈՒՆԿՆ
etymology: = Native Armenian word: from the Hn. ōsn- or usn-form. Its cognates are Znd. uši "two ears. 2. intelligence, understanding" (from which Ps. [arabic word] hōš "intelligence, mind"> Hn. loanword uhs), Yn. οῦς, Dor. ὥς, Lt. auris (which is <*ausis, which is very well derived from aus-cultō "listen">Fr. écouter "to listen"), Goth. ausō, Hbg. ora Germ. Ohr, Hol. oor, Hn. eyra, Eng. éare, Eng. ear, Lit. ausis, Lat. auss. Hn. ausins, Hsl. ucho (gen. ušese, gen. աši), Czech. ucho, uši, Russian. уϰо, уаи, hirl. áu, o, Albanian veš, all meaning "ear" (Pokorny 1, 18, Trautmann 19, Boisacq 731, Walde 76, Ernout-Meillet 88-9, Kluge 355). The proto-form was Hn. aus, us, ōus, ōs, which probably derives from the original root au "to hear". In Armenian, the root, growing with the nasal n, became first osn- or usn-, which gave ūn-(a similar growth is also shown by Yn. ούατος <*aus-n-t). and then adding the -kn participle (as a participle? cf. mouse, fish, arm, and or similar to the word derived from akn, according to Meillet ZАPh 1, 147), it resulted in unkn, as u-n-kn. -Hyubsh. 484.

title: ՍՏՈՒԱՐ
etymology: = Borrowed from the word Phl. stavar "thick, thick, solid", with which cf. also Psr. [arabic word] ustuvār "strong, firm", Kurdish. stur, ustūr "thick, thick, strong", Russian. st'ur "big, large, mighty", Baloch. istūr, Afghan. star "big, wide, large", Znd. stawra-"strong, powerful", stavah-"stuarity, thickness", stū-styi-"stuar", Sans. sthāvará-"stuar. thick, firm", sthurá-"thick, solid", stūla-"thick, thick" etc. The cognates of these words are Jn. σταυρός "pole", στεδμαι "to stand on, to confirm, to validate", Lt. re-staurare "to restore". in-staurare "to stand, to establish", Goth. stiurjan "to establish", Hbg. stūri "strong", stiura "stake", stiuren "to govern, to support", Lit. storas "thick", Hist. staurr "stake" and others, whose proto-form is Hn. stēu-, stāu- "to stand, to remain firmly on one's feet" and -ro-particle: Hn. steuro-, stəuro-, stūro-, steuero-, which supposedly would have given native Armenian *stoyr, *stor, *stur, *stor. - Against the Iranian -av-form, Hn. sometimes we find -ov-. cf. Khos-rov<xusrav and dsrov<dusrav.-The simplest root of all is stā-"to stand", for its numerous derivatives see Pokorny 2, 603-609, also Boisacq 902 and hl. under the word sta-nal. Of course, it is also from Iranian. ostavan "strong, solid, stingy

title: ՎԱՇԽ
etymology: = Phl. [other alphabet] [arabic word] vaxš “money-bringing toevo-sye”. The root means “increased, increased, grown”, cf. phl. I vaxšttan “grow”, [arabic word] νaxšišn “growing, greatness”, nd. [arabic word] ︎ vaxšaiti “to grow”, uxšyeiti “to grow”, sans. [other alphabet] ︎ [other alphabet] vakšati, [other alphabet] vakšayati “to grow”. The proto-form of these is the ancient aveq-s, which is a-tsed from the simple root aveg-, aug- “to grow”, the other cognates of which see հանանան. In Armenian the native form is հանանան.-Hyupsh. 243.

title: ՎԱՇՏ
etymology: = Must be borrowed from a word meaning "army" in Phl. *vašk. Such a word is not preserved in Iranian literature, but there is Phl. gast (old vašt) "became", vaštan "to become", which on the basis of the Iranian rt>št sound change gives rise to vartītan, gartītan "to become", vartisn, gartišn "twist, circle", Sans. vart, vartate "to become", Prs. [arabic word] gar-dīdan "to turn, to turn, to turn", [arabic word] gāštan "to turn", [arabic word] gardana> from the word гртнак (=lt. verto "to turn", Old High German werdan, German werden "to become, to be", R. вepηetь "to turn, to turn, to turn, to pierce", вepηeл "skewer", Lit. verčiu, versti "to turn", etc.). The root of all is Hn. vert- "to turn, to make" (Pokorny 1, 274, Horn § 886). The words denoting "group" in general are related to the idea of "all". qu. regiment, bar, battalion, camp ("maneak. 2. army" <pl. čambar "circle"), Tchk top, lt. circulus, fr. cercle, rus. круръ, коужокъ, following which also the working circle "cloak, round. 2. group of people".-Ach.

title: ՎԵՅ
etymology: = Native Armenian word, whose cognates are: sans. šat, šaš, znd. [other alphabet] ︎ xšvaš, phl. ❇ šaš, prs.. ❇ šaš, afghan. špaž, oss. äxsäz, kurd. šeš, toxhar. skas, yn. čς, fέé, ut. sex. ital. sei, fran. six, rum. sese, goth. saihs, hbg. sêhs, ger. sechs, hol. zes, eng. six, hhyus. sex, hsl. šest', rus. шeсть. lit. šeši, lett. seši, kymr. chwech, hirll. sé, alban. gašte, all "six". their antecedents are assumed to be k'suek's, suek's, sek's, k'sek's, k'uek's. ueks. from the latter are especially lit. uszšs "six weeks of the birth", hebrew. uschts "sixth". The proto-form of the Armenian word has also been assumed in various ways. thus Hubsh, Armen. Stud. § 266 puts suek's, Meil-let MSL 7, 162 k'svek's, the first three consonants of which are clarified, leaving only the third, Hubsh. Arm. Gram. 495 and Meillet MSL 10, 280 uek's, Pedersen KZ 38, 229 šuek's, Bartholomae, Studien 2, 34 assumes an Iranian influence in the word veshtasan, finally Meillet, Dial. in-do-eur. 86 puts the old Armenian form *vech, from which vesh- and vesh have emerged. Another comment on the phonetic relations is in Grammont MSL 20, 216. Because the initial su gives the hy. k, and u>g, therefore the form k'suek's is preferable of all these (Pokorny 2, 522, Walde 707. Trautmann 144, Boisacq 260, Ernout-Meillet 894, Horn § 783). -Hyubsh. 495.

title: ՑԱԽ
etymology: =A native Armenian word, the antecedent of which (at least in terms of the initial sound) is imprecise. The cognates are stns. çákha-, prs. [arabic word] ︎ sax lit. šaká, hsl. sakù, kymr cainc, mirl. gēc, nirl. gēag "branch, twig", lit., sakēsis, hprus. sagnis, lett. sakne "root", goth. hōha. "stem" and perhaps hsl. soxa, bulg. sochá, rus. coxá "rod, stick, stem" (Pokorny 1, 335, Trautmann 297). The antecedent of these is k'ak, which also has its own rang-gai form k'ank. For Armenian, Bar-tbolomae Stud. 2, 41 assumes hnkh. k'hakhā, Meillet MSL 8, 294 i 9. 323 The word assumes two forms, one of which is represented by Armenian and Persian, and the other by Sanskrit. Finally, Petersson, Ar. u. Arm Stud. 98 places the Armenian proto-form as khnkh. sk'ə-kho.-Hyupsh. 499.

title: ՓԵՌԵԿ
etymology: = Native Armenian word: from the root spher- "to split, to cut". cognate with φπααλοοω "to tear", οπάραγμα "torn piece", ϰπαραγμός "torn", H. spigrr (*sperrō) "torn piece of cloth". In Avestan -ek can be a particle and or from the suffix -g-. Several forms are assumed with this suffix (sp-hereg-). Some of them mean "to burst, to crack". Thus, in Greek σφαρϰγεῖσϑαι, Sanskrit sphūrjati, Lit. spragéti, H. spraka, Latin sprég-stu, Frisian sprockeln (Boisacq 891, 927, Pokorny 2, 668 and 673. The latter also gives many other words whose meaning does not allow them to be combined with prefixes, as Ernout-Meiuet 919 also says).

title: ՕՁ
etymology: = Native Armenian word: from the form ang*hi- from the cognates hmt. yn. άβεις (Hessian), lt. anguis, pt. angis, lit. angis, lt. uodze, pt. unc, hsl. *9ži, rus. ужъ, polish. waž, irl. -ung (found in the word esc-ung "eel", which is compounded with the word esr "lake" and originally means "lake snake"). These words mean "snake or viper" and or both. In the proto-language there were two similar forms: eg*hi-and ang*hi-. In Armenian, viper (<eg*. hi-) is derived from the first and snake from the second. A group of words meaning "eel" also belong here. rk. lt. anguilla, lit. ungurys. hsl. ag-orišti, Polish wegorz, Hebrew angur-gis (Walde 42, Pokorny 1, 63-65, Traut-mann 8, Boisacq 213, Ernout-Meillet 49). The Greek ang*hi-form was opposed to the Greek "ang", but "snake" is one of those words that has undergone many transformations in the Indo-European cognates. For the word "snake" the cognates form numerous cognates, which retain one form or another. Thus, Sans. áhi-, Znd. aži-, Hebrew Izh. yn. ὄφις form a group.-Goth. nadre, Hisl. naδr, Gallic neidr, Hebrew nātra, Hiss. nādra, German natter, Irish nathir, and the Western cognates form the B.-Lt. anguis, Հայ. Տանական and n constitute the third group. - In addition to this, in some groups there are suffixes instead of the word "Տանական" itself. For example, "serpent" (Latin ser pens), "land" (Hul. zmija). "green" (Lithuanian Zaltys), "disgusting" (Hul. gadú) and n. All this shows that the snake was tabooed and that each language tried in some way to avoid the runic name, either by modifying it or by inventing a new name (see Meillet, Interdictions de Vocabulaire, pp. 13-14). In Armenian, the ancient form ang*hi- was modified into *ang'hi-. from which snake could have originated. -Hubsh. 426.
